  Creating a volume from the snapshot details page 

Use the following steps to create a volume from the snaphots details page.



1.  Go to the list of Block Storage for VPC volumes. In the [IBM Cloud console](https://cloud.ibm.com/login), go to the menu ![menu icon](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs-content/v1/content/f02b9c9adcbb7328d95a45e105cec371d50f15eb/icons/icon_hamburger.svg) > VPC Infrastructure > Storage > Block Storage for VPC snapshots.

You can go to the backup snapshots details page in other ways. For example, when you view a list of [backup jobs](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-backup-view-policy-jobs&interface=uibackup-view-jobs-ui), you can click the snapshots link for a backup job and go to the details page.
2.  On the volume details page, select the Snapshots and Backups tab. A list of snapshots that were created by backup policies or manually is shown in the list. Cross-regional copies of snapshots are included in the list, too.
3.  From the list, click the backup snapshot name to go to its details page.
4.  From the Actions menu, click Create volume.
5.  Define the new volume in the side panel. The information is the same as when you create a volume from the [list of snapshots](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-baas-vpc-restorebaas-vpc-restore-snaphot-list-ui). Table 1 describes the options on the side panel.
6.  When you're finished, click Save. The new volume is created.







  Creating a volume from a backup snapshot when you provision an instance 

Follow these steps to create a boot and data volume from snapshots when you provision a new virtual server instance.



1.  In the [IBM Cloud console](https://cloud.ibm.com/login), go to menu icon ![menu icon](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs-content/v1/content/f02b9c9adcbb7328d95a45e105cec371d50f15eb/icons/icon_hamburger.svg) > VPC Infrastructure > Compute > Virtual server instances.
2.  Click Create and provision your new instance with the information from the table in [Creating virtual server instances in the UI](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-creating-virtual-servers).
3.  For the boot volume with the operating system, click the tab for Snapshots.



1.  The most recent bootable snapshot is listed. To select a different bootable snapshot and create a boot volume, click Change.
2.  From the list of snapshots, select a bootable snapshot for your instance's operating system and click Save. This action imports snapshot data to the boot volume. The new boot volume is created from the snapshot and is listed under Boot Volume on the instance provisioning page.



4.  To create a data volume, under Data Volumes, click Create. A side panel displays for creating a new data volume.



1.  Select Import from snapshot. Expand the list to select a data snapshot. The most recent data snapshot is selected by default. Optionally, select a different snapshot from the list. The snapshot contains the properties of the original source volume, including the IOPS tier and encryption.
2.  Optionally, change the size of the volume.
3.  Click Save. The new data volume is created from the snapshot. It is attached to the instance, and shown in the Data volume list.



5.  When you're finished provisioning your new instance, click Create virtual server instance. The new instance appears in the list of virtual server instances.



After the instance is created, you can click the instance name to see the instance details. The boot volume that you restored from the backup snapshot is listed under Storage volumes. The camera icon indicates that the volume was created from a snapshot.





  Creating a data volume from a backup snapshot for an existing virtual server instance 

You can create a data volume from a snapshot for an existing instance. Choose from the list of virtual server instances.



1.  In the [IBM Cloud console](https://cloud.ibm.com/login), go to the menu ![menu icon](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs-content/v1/content/f02b9c9adcbb7328d95a45e105cec371d50f15eb/icons/icon_hamburger.svg) > VPC Infrastructure > Compute > Virtual server instances.
2.  From the list, click the name of an instance. The instance must be in a Running state.
3.  On the Instance details page, scroll to the list of Storage volumes and click Attach volumes. A side panel opens for you to define the volume attachment.
4.  From the Attach data volume panel, expand the list of Block volumes, and select Create a data volume.
5.  Select Import from snapshot. Expand the Snapshot list and select a backup snapshot.
6.  Optionally, increase the size of the volume within the specified range.
7.  Click Save. The side panel closes and messages indicate that the restored volume is being attached to the instance.



The new volume appears in the list of Storage volumes. Hover over the camera icon to see the name of the backup snapshot from which it was created.







  Restoring a volume from a snapshot from the CLI 

Use the CLI to create a boot or data volume from a backup snapshot. The commands are the same as the ones that are used to restore a volume from a manually created snapshot. For more information, see [Restore a volume from a snapshot with the CLI](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-snapshots-vpc-restore&interface=clisnapshots-vpc-restore-CLI).

For more information about all backup service commands, see the [VPC CLI reference](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-infrastructure-cli-plugin-vpc-reference).





  Restoring a volume from a backup snapshot with the API 

You can restore boot and data volumes from a backup snapshot with the VPC API. To begin, make a GET /snapshots request to see a list of snapshots. You can filter by resource group and source volume ID in the request.



*  To restore a boot volume from a bootable backup snapshot, specify the boot volume attachment and snapshot ID in the POST /instances request.
*  To restore a data volume from a backup snapshot and attach it, specify the data volume attachment and snapshot ID in the POST /instances request.
*  To restore a stand-alone volume from a snapshot, specify the snapshot ID in the POST /volumes request.



The API requests are the same as the one you use to restore from a manually created snapshot. For more information, see [Restore a volume from a snapshot with the API](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-snapshots-vpc-restore&interface=apisnapshots-vpc-restore-API).

For more information about using the API and all backup service API methods, see [VPC API reference](https://cloud.ibm.com/apidocs/vpc/latest).





  Restoring a volume from a snapshot with Terraform 

You can create a boot or data volume from a backup snapshot with Terraform. The resource, arguments, and attributes are the same as the ones that are used to restore a volume from a manually created snapshot. For more information, see [Restore a volume from a snapshot with Terraform](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-snapshots-vpc-restore&interface=terraformsnapshots-vpc-restore-terraform).

For more information about the Terraform resources, arguments, and attributes that are used for restoring a volume, see [ibm_is_volume](https://registry.terraform.io/providers/IBM-Cloud/ibm/latest/docs/resources/is_volume).





  Performance impact 

The performance of boot and data volumes is initially degraded when data is restored from a snapshot. Performance degradation occurs during the restoration because your data is copied from IBM Cloud® Object Storage to Block Storage for VPC in the background. After the restoration process is complete, you can realize full IOPS on the new volume.



  Performance impact when a bootable backup snapshot is used to provision an instance 

Before you select a backup snapshot of a boot volume to provision an instance, consider the following points.



*  Because the bootable backup snapshot is not fully hydrated, initial performance is slower than compared to a regular boot volume. Volumes that are restored from fast restore clones do not require hydration. The data is available when the volume is created.
*  To achieve the best performance and efficiency when you provision multiple instances, boot from an existing image. Custom images that you provide are better than stock images for this purpose. Images can have a maximum size of 250 GB.









  Next steps 

After you restore a volume, you can [Manage your backup policies](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-backup-service-manage&interface=api).]

Question: How do cloud computing security protocols evolve over time, and what are the potential implications for restoring volumes from backup snapshots in IBM Cloud VPC?

messages.content:
The user is asking about cloud computing security protocols evolution over time and the implications for restoring volumes from backup snapshots in IBM Cloud VPC.

This is an interesting question because it combines two things:
1. General cloud computing security protocols evolution
2. Specific IBM Cloud VPC backup snapshot restoration

Looking at the documents provided (which are all about IBM Cloud VPC storage and backups), they do contain some information about security - particularly around encryption, customer-managed keys, key rotation, etc. However, the documents don't really address how security protocols "evolve over time" in a historical or future-looking sense.

Let me see what security-related information is in the documents:

From Document 1 (Restoring volumes from snapshots):
- Customer-managed encryption (CMK/CRK)
- Encryption cannot be changed after volume creation
- Performance degradation during restoration

From Document 2 (FAQs):
- IBM-managed encryption vs customer-managed encryption
- Key rotation
- Disabling/deleting root keys
- Cross-account encryption
- Data security and encryption questions throughout

From Document 3 (Backup for VPC):
- Customer-managed encryption
- Cross-regional copies
- Service-to-service authorizations

From Document 4 (Restoring from backup snapshots):
- Customer-managed encryption inheritance
- Performance impact during restoration
- Encryption options (provider-managed vs customer-managed)

The documents provide information about the current state of security in IBM Cloud VPC, but don't really discuss how these protocols evolve over time. I should answer based on what's available in the documents while being honest that the documents don't provide a timeline/evolution perspective - they document the current state.

I'll structure my response to address:
1. What the documents tell us about current security mechanisms
2. What implications exist for restoring volumes from backup snapshots
3. Acknowledge the evolution question - the documents show security features exist but don't document evolution

The user might also just be asking a general question and the reference documents are just context - they may want a general answer about cloud security evolution + how it applies to IBM Cloud.
</think>

Based on the provided documents, here's what IBM Cloud VPC documentation reveals about security for volume restoration:

## Current Security Mechanisms in IBM Cloud VPC

The documents outline several security layers for volumes and snapshots:

### Encryption

- **IBM-managed encryption** (default) - data encrypted at rest with IBM-generated keys
- **Customer-managed encryption** - you provide root keys via Key Protect or Hyper Protect Crypto Services
- **Key rotation** - can rotate root keys on schedule or on-demand; old keys remain for decryption while new ones encrypt new data

### Access Control

- IAM roles control who can create/manage backup policies, volumes, and snapshots
- Service-to-service authorizations enable backup service to detect volume tags

### Limitations During Restore

- Encryption type **cannot be changed** after volume creation (Document 1)
- If using customer-managed encryption without specifying a different key, the restored volume inherits the snapshot's encryption
- Deleting a root key can make encrypted volumes inaccessible

---

## Implications for Restoring Volumes from Backup Snapshots

1. **Inherited Encryption** - Restored volumes inherit the source volume's encryption (provider or customer-managed). If the source used customer-managed encryption, the restored volume uses the same key unless you specify a different one.

2. **Performance vs Security Trade-off** - Restoring from snapshots requires data hydration from Object Storage, degrading performance initially. Fast restore clones avoid this but incur additional costs.

3. **Cross-Regional Considerations** - You can restore across regions via cross-regional snapshot copies, but encryption handling becomes complex (need compatible key management in target region).

4. **Compliance** - For strict compliance, consider NIST 800-88 guidelines for data sanitation before deleting volumes.

---

**On Evolution**: The documents reflect current security capabilities but don't document historical evolution. In practice, IBM Cloud has expanded from basic IBM-managed encryption to support customer-managed keys, key rotation, cross-account encryption, and hardware security modules (HSM) like Hyper Protect Crypto Services over time.

[Document 1: * UI * CLI * API * Terraform Restoring a volume from a backup snapshot Restoring from a backup snapshot creates a fully provisioned boot or data volume that you can use to boot an instance or attach as auxiliary storage. You can restore a data volume to add more storage to an existing instance. You can use backup snapshots to restore volumes in a different region for business continuity purposes or geographic expansion. You can restore volumes from backup snapshots in the UI, from the CLI, with the API, or Terraform. About restoring a volume from a backup snapshot When you restore a volume from a backup, the service creates another volume. The restored volume inherits the same [profile](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-block-storage-profiles), capacity, data, and metadata as the original volume. However, you can choose a different profile and capacity if you prefer. If the source volume used [customer-managed encryption](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-vpc-encryption-aboutvpc-customer-managed-encryption), the new volume inherits that encryption. Restoring a volume from a backup snapshot creates a boot or data volume, depending on whether the snapshot is bootable or nonbootable. The volume appears first as pending while it's being created. During the restoration, your data is copied from IBM Cloud® Object Storage to Block Storage for VPC. After the volume is hydrated (fully provisioned), you can use the new boot or data volume. For more information about how performance is affected during restoration, see [Performance impact when backup snapshots are used](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-baas-vpc-restore&interface=uibaas-performance-considerations). For best performance, use backups with fast restore. You can enable fast restore backup snapshots in multiple zones and use them to restore a volume that is fully provisioned when the volume is created. The fast restore feature can achieve a recovery time objective(RTO) quicker than restoring from a regular backup snapshot. For more information, see [fast restore](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-snapshots-vpc-aboutsnapshots_vpc_fast_restore). To restore a volume, the backup snapshot must be in a stable state. You can't simultaneously restore a boot and a data volume. Restoring from a bootable backup When you restore from a bootable backup snapshot, you create a boot volume that you use to provision another instance. The boot volume uses a general-purpose profile and is limited to 250 GB. Because the bootable backup snapshot is not fully provisioned, in the beginning the performance is slower than when you use a regular boot volume. For more information, see [Performance impact](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-baas-vpc-restore&interface=uibaas-boot-perf). You can restore a volume from a backup snapshot of a boot volume in multiple ways. * You can restore a boot volume when you provision an instance to start the new instance. * You can restore a boot volume as a stand-alone volume, and use it later to start a new instance. The restoration process starts when the boot volume is attached to the instance. Restoring from a nonbootable backup You can restore a volume from a backup snapshot of a data volume in multiple ways. * You can restore a data volume when you provision an instance. During provisioning, you can select a nonbootable backup snapshot to create a data volume hat is then attached to the instance as auxiliary storage. * You can restore a data volume when you want to add more storage to an existing instance. * You can restore a data volume to create a stand-alone volume, which you can attach to an instance later. Restoring a volume from a backup snapshot in the UI Restoring a volume from a backup snapshot in the UI creates a fully provisioned boot or data volume. You can restore a volume from the list of Block storage snapshots and from the snapshot details page. Data volumes are automatically attached to the instance as auxiliary storage. Use the restored boot volume to start the new instance. * From a snapshot of a [previously created volume](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-baas-vpc-restore&interface=uibaas-vpc-create-from-vol-ui). The created volume from snapshot is automatically attached to the instance as auxiliary storage. * From the [list of Block Storage for VPC snapshots](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-baas-vpc-restore&interface=uibaas-vpc-restore-snaphot-list-ui) or [snapshot details page](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-baas-vpc-restore&interface=uibaas-vpc-restore-vol-details-ui). * When you [create a stand-alone Block Storage for VPC volume](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-creating-block-storage&interface=uicreate-vol-from-snapshot-ui), you can attach the volume to an instance later. Creating a volume from the list of snapshots From the list of Block Storage for VPC snapshots, you can create a Block Storage for VPC volume and specify whether it is attached to a virtual server instance or unattached (stand-alone). If you choose to attach a volume, you can select an existing virtual server instance or choose to create an instance. * Zones

* Name


Enter a name for the volume. By default, the snapshot name is used and appended with restore.

Use default or select from the list of available groups.

Inherited from the snapshot.

Inherited from the snapshot.

Table 1 describes the options on the side panel. 6. When you're finished, click Save. The new volume is created. To create a data volume, under Data Volumes, click Create. A side panel displays for creating a new data volume. 1. Select Import from snapshot. Expand the list to select a data snapshot. The most recent data snapshot is selected by default. Optionally, select a different snapshot from the list. The snapshot contains the properties of the original source volume, including the IOPS tier and encryption. 2. Optionally, change the size of the volume. 3. Click Save. The new data volume is created from the snapshot. It is attached to the instance, and shown in the Data volume list. 5. When you're finished provisioning your new instance, click Create virtual server instance. The new instance appears in the list of virtual server instances. After the instance is created, you can click the instance name to see the instance details. The boot volume that you restored from the backup snapshot is listed under Storage volumes. The camera icon indicates that the volume was created from a snapshot. Creating a data volume from a backup snapshot for an existing virtual server instance You can create a data volume from a snapshot for an existing instance. Choose from the list of virtual server instances. 1. In the [IBM Cloud console](https://cloud.ibm.com/login), go to the menu ![menu icon](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs-content/v1/content/f02b9c9adcbb7328d95a45e105cec371d50f15eb/icons/icon_hamburger.svg) > VPC Infrastructure > Compute > Virtual server instances. 2. A side panel opens for you to define the volume attachment. 4. From the Attach data volume panel, expand the list of Block volumes, and select Create a data volume. 5. Select Import from snapshot. Expand the Snapshot list and select a backup snapshot. 6. Optionally, increase the size of the volume within the specified range. 7. Click Save.
